evals.name: viewmodel-simple
evals.expected_output: ViewModel imports UIKit — this violates the core MVVM rule that ViewModels must be platform-agnostic. Importing UIKit: (1) Prevents unit testing without a simulator/device (UIKit requires a running UI environment), (2) Couples the ViewModel to iOS platform, blocking reuse on macOS/visionOS, (3) Signals the ViewModel knows about presentation concerns. Fix: expose `avatarName: String` instead of `UIImage`, return `String` from formatting methods instead of `NSAttributedString`.

assertions.text: Identifies UIKit import as the core violation
assertions.rationale: Rule #1 from skill: ViewModels never import UIKit

assertions.text: Explains this breaks unit testability (UIKit requires simulator)
assertions.rationale: Skill specifically states UIKit import prevents unit testing — baseline may only mention platform coupling

assertions.text: Recommends exposing platform-agnostic types (String for avatar name, String for formatted text)
assertions.rationale: Correct fix: expose iconName/imageName and plain String

assertions.text: Identifies missing private(set) on @Published properties as a separate violation (unidirectional data flow not enforced)
assertions.rationale: Skill flags private(set) as a distinct violation beyond the UIKit import — baseline typically focuses only on the import issue

evals.name: viewmodel-medium
evals.expected_output: Separate boolean flags allow impossible states: `isLoading = true` AND `errorMessage != nil` simultaneously, or `isEmpty = true` AND `products.count > 0`. The ViewController must handle combinations of four independent publishers, making the switch logic complex and bug-prone. Fix: use a `ViewState<T>` enum with mutually exclusive cases — `.loading`, `.loaded([Product])`, `.empty`, `.error(String)`. One `@Published private(set) var state: ViewState<[Product]>` replaces all four properties.

assertions.text: Identifies separate boolean flags allow impossible states (e.g., isLoading + errorMessage simultaneously)
assertions.rationale: Impossible state argument is the skill's specific rationale for ViewState — baseline may note the complexity without naming the impossibility

assertions.text: Recommends ViewState<T> enum with mutually exclusive cases (.loading, .loaded, .empty, .error)
assertions.rationale: ViewState<T> is the skill-prescribed fix — baseline often suggests just combining booleans, not an enum

assertions.text: Notes one @Published property replaces four
assertions.rationale: Demonstrates the simplification ViewState provides

assertions.text: Shows impossible states table or lists specific invalid combinations (e.g., isLoading=true AND errorMessage!=nil simultaneously)
assertions.rationale: Skill response enumerates concrete impossible state combinations in a table — baseline typically describes the problem abstractly without listing specific combinations

assertions.text: Warns about race conditions between separate publisher emissions (intermediate inconsistent state between CombineLatest updates)
assertions.rationale: Skill identifies that separate @Published properties cause intermediate glitch states between emissions — baseline focuses on logical impossibility but misses the temporal race

evals.name: viewmodel-complex
evals.expected_output: Four violations ranked by severity: (1) 🔴 Critical: UITableView reference in ViewModel — ViewModel owns a UI object, creates a retain cycle (VM → tableView → VC → VM), couples to UIKit. Remove entirely; VC observes @Published state. (2) 🔴 Critical: tableView?.reloadData() called from ViewModel — ViewModel must never call UI methods; state change propagates via Combine bindings. (3) 🟡 High: VC writes ViewModel state directly (viewModel.orders = [], viewModel.isLoading = true) — state properties must be `private(set)`, VC only forwards user actions. (4) 🟢 Medium: Separate isLoading/errorMessage booleans instead of ViewState<T> enum — allows impossible states.

assertions.text: Flags UITableView reference in ViewModel as Critical — creates retain cycle and UIKit coupling
assertions.rationale: VM holding a UI reference creates the VM→UITableView→VC→VM retain cycle — skill-specific anti-pattern

assertions.text: Flags tableView?.reloadData() called from ViewModel as Critical
assertions.rationale: ViewModel must never call UI methods — all updates via Combine

assertions.text: Identifies VC writing ViewModel state as a High violation — properties must be private(set)
assertions.rationale: private(set) enforcement for unidirectional data flow is a key skill rule

assertions.text: Identifies separate boolean flags (isLoading + errorMessage) as a Medium violation — use ViewState<T>
assertions.rationale: Impossible state pattern from skill

evals.name: combine-bindings-simple
evals.expected_output: Yes — retain cycle. The exact path is: self (FeedViewController) → cancellables (Set<AnyCancellable>) → AnyCancellable → closure → self. The closure captures self strongly, and self owns cancellables which owns the closure. Neither can be deallocated. Fix: add [weak self] and guard or optional chain: `.sink { [weak self] posts in self?.tableView.reloadData() }`.

assertions.text: Confirms there is a retain cycle
assertions.rationale: Core sink retain cycle

assertions.text: Traces the exact cycle path: self → cancellables → AnyCancellable → closure → self
assertions.rationale: Skill documents this exact path — baseline may say 'strong capture' without tracing the cancellables chain

assertions.text: Fixes with [weak self] in the sink closure
assertions.rationale: Standard fix

assertions.text: Identifies missing .receive(on: DispatchQueue.main) as an additional issue — sink updates UIKit from potentially background thread
assertions.rationale: Skill response catches the threading issue in addition to the retain cycle — baseline focuses solely on the memory leak

evals.name: combine-bindings-medium
evals.expected_output: Two issues: (1) Binding in init — at this point titleLabel does not exist yet (outlets are nil until viewDidLoad). Calling self?.titleLabel.text will silently fail or crash depending on whether it's a force-unwrap. Move all bindings to viewDidLoad via a setupBindings() method. (2) Missing .receive(on: DispatchQueue.main) — the service layer may deliver on a background thread; titleLabel.text must be set on the main thread. Add .receive(on: DispatchQueue.main) before .sink.

assertions.text: Identifies binding in init causes nil outlet access (views not loaded until viewDidLoad)
assertions.rationale: Skill rule: bind in viewDidLoad, never in init — baseline often focuses only on weak self

assertions.text: Recommends moving bindings to viewDidLoad via setupBindings()
assertions.rationale: Skill-prescribed pattern: setupBindings() called from viewDidLoad

assertions.text: Identifies missing .receive(on: DispatchQueue.main) before UI update in sink
assertions.rationale: Skill rule: always .receive(on: .main) before UI updates — background thread UI mutation

assertions.text: Explains @Published fires immediately on subscription (CurrentValueSubject behavior) so the nil-outlet access happens during init, not just on future updates
assertions.rationale: Skill explains @Published replays current value to new subscribers — the binding in init fires immediately with nil outlets, not just on subsequent changes. Baseline misses this subtlety.

evals.name: combine-bindings-complex
evals.expected_output: Two issues: (1) Sink not stored in cancellables — the AnyCancellable returned by sink is immediately deallocated (not stored), so the subscription is cancelled before any values arrive. Fix: .store(in: &cancellables). (2) PassthroughSubject for stateful results instead of @Published — PassthroughSubject is for one-shot events (e.g., navigation, alerts). Subscribers who connect after a value was sent miss it. New subscribers (e.g., after view reappears) need the current results. Use @Published private(set) var results: [SearchResult] = [] so subscribers always get the current value on subscription.

assertions.text: CRITICAL: Identifies sink not stored in cancellables — subscription immediately cancelled
assertions.rationale: Missing .store is the exact reason no updates arrive — skill makes this the first thing to check

assertions.text: Identifies PassthroughSubject is wrong for stateful data — use @Published instead
assertions.rationale: Skill distinguishes PassthroughSubject (one-shot events) vs @Published (state) — baseline often accepts PassthroughSubject for all Combine usage

assertions.text: Explains @Published replays current value to new subscribers; PassthroughSubject does not
assertions.rationale: Key behavioral difference explaining why new subscribers miss values

evals.name: coordinator-simple
evals.expected_output: ViewController instantiating and pushing another ViewController is a MVVM anti-pattern. ProductListViewController now knows about ProductDetailViewController — coupling two screens together. This prevents reusing either screen in different flows and makes navigation untestable. Fix: ViewController calls `viewModel.onProductSelected?(product)` via a closure. The Coordinator sets this closure and handles VC creation and navigation. VCs must never instantiate other VCs.

assertions.text: Identifies VC instantiating another VC as the violation
assertions.rationale: Navigation is Coordinator's responsibility from skill

assertions.text: Recommends ViewModel navigation closure (onProductSelected?) that Coordinator sets
assertions.rationale: ViewModel signals navigation intent via closures — skill's specific pattern vs generic 'use a delegate'

evals.name: coordinator-medium
evals.expected_output: AuthCoordinator is never removed from childCoordinators after it finishes — memory leak. The AuthCoordinator and everything it owns (ViewModels, ViewControllers) remain allocated. Fix: implement the didFinish pattern. AuthCoordinator exposes a closure: `var didFinish: ((AuthCoordinator) -> Void)?`. AppCoordinator sets it before appending: `authCoord.didFinish = { [weak self] coord in self?.childCoordinators.removeAll { $0 === coord } }`. AuthCoordinator calls `didFinish?(self)` in finishAuth().

assertions.text: Identifies AuthCoordinator never removed from childCoordinators — memory leak
assertions.rationale: Child coordinator lifecycle from coordinator-navigation.md

assertions.text: Recommends didFinish closure pattern: coordinator calls didFinish?(self), parent removes it
assertions.rationale: Exact didFinish coordinator cleanup pattern from skill — baseline may suggest a delegate protocol instead of the closure approach

assertions.text: Shows removeAll { $0 === coord } to remove by identity from childCoordinators
assertions.rationale: Identity-based removal is the correct mechanism for reference types

evals.name: coordinator-complex
evals.expected_output: Three issues: (1) Coordinator strongly holds profileVC — UINavigationController already retains pushed VCs. This double-retain prevents VC deallocation after popping. Never store VC references in coordinators. (2) settingsCoord.didFinish not set — SettingsCoordinator will never be removed from childCoordinators, leaking it and all its VCs. (3) ProfileCoordinator not set as UINavigationControllerDelegate — the coordinator has no way to detect when the user taps the Back button. Without this, child coordinators aren't cleaned up when the user navigates back natively. Wire: `navigationController.delegate = self` and implement `navigationController(_:didShow:animated:)`.

assertions.text: Identifies Coordinator storing strong VC reference prevents deallocation after pop
assertions.rationale: navController already retains VCs — double retain is a coordinator-specific leak from skill

assertions.text: Identifies missing didFinish on settingsCoord — SettingsCoordinator never cleaned up
assertions.rationale: Child coordinator lifecycle from coordinator-navigation.md

assertions.text: Identifies missing UINavigationControllerDelegate — back button taps undetected, child coordinators not cleaned up
assertions.rationale: UINavigationControllerDelegate for back-button cleanup is a key coordinator pattern from skill

evals.name: dependency-injection-simple
evals.expected_output: ViewModel accesses NetworkService.shared (singleton) directly — hidden dependency, tightly coupled to a concrete type. This makes the ViewModel untestable (can't inject a mock). Fix: inject via constructor using a protocol type: `init(service: NetworkServiceProtocol)`. The Coordinator or factory creates the ViewModel with the real service; tests pass a MockNetworkService.

assertions.text: Identifies singleton access as a hidden dependency preventing testability
assertions.rationale: Constructor injection rule from dependency-injection.md

assertions.text: Recommends protocol-based constructor injection: init(service: NetworkServiceProtocol)
assertions.rationale: Skill prescribes protocol types specifically, not just 'use init injection' — enables mock injection in tests

assertions.text: Shows Coordinator or factory as the injection site — Coordinator creates ViewModel with real service
assertions.rationale: Skill wires DI through the Coordinator layer (coordinator creates VM with real service, tests use mock) — baseline typically omits who creates the ViewModel

evals.name: dependency-injection-medium
evals.expected_output: Two issues: (1) viewModel is optional — if the coordinator forgets to set it, viewDidLoad silently fails. Prefer a non-optional property set before viewDidLoad runs. (2) Storyboard VCs can't use constructor injection via a custom init directly with instantiateViewController(withIdentifier:). Better approaches: (a) Use instantiateViewController(creator:) (iOS 13+) to call a custom init with non-optional dependencies, (b) Migrate to programmatic VC creation to enable full constructor injection. If property injection is kept (e.g., for backward compatibility), assert non-nil in viewDidLoad rather than optional-chaining silently.

assertions.text: Identifies optional viewModel allows silent failure if coordinator forgets to set it
assertions.rationale: Non-optional dependencies via constructor are the skill's standard

assertions.text: Explains instantiateViewController(withIdentifier:) prevents custom init — property injection limitation of Storyboards
assertions.rationale: Skill covers Storyboard DI limitations specifically — baseline may not know about instantiateViewController(creator:)

assertions.text: Recommends instantiateViewController(creator:) (iOS 13+) or programmatic VC creation as fixes
assertions.rationale: instantiateViewController(creator:) is the iOS 13+ Storyboard DI solution from dependency-injection.md

evals.name: dependency-injection-complex
evals.expected_output: The Coordinator is doing dependency construction — it should only do navigation and screen wiring. Fix: introduce a ScreenFactory protocol injected into the Coordinator via constructor. The factory encapsulates VC+VM creation with pre-configured dependencies. OrderCoordinator becomes: `func showOrderDetail(id: UUID) { let vc = factory.makeOrderDetail(id: id); navController.pushViewController(vc, animated: true) }`. This makes the Coordinator testable with a MockScreenFactory and moves dependency setup to one place (the AppCompositionRoot or DI container).

assertions.text: Identifies Coordinator doing dependency construction — violates single responsibility
assertions.rationale: Coordinator responsibility is navigation, not dependency wiring

assertions.text: Recommends ScreenFactory protocol injected into Coordinator — factory creates VC+VM pairs
assertions.rationale: Factory pattern for VC+VM creation is the skill-prescribed solution from dependency-injection.md

assertions.text: Notes MockScreenFactory makes the Coordinator independently testable
assertions.rationale: Testability through factory injection is the key benefit — baseline often stops at 'extract a factory' without the testability angle

evals.name: viewstate-simple
evals.expected_output: Bind in viewDidLoad via setupBindings(). Use a switch over state in the sink closure: .loading shows activityIndicator, hides tableView/emptyView/errorView. .loaded(items) hides indicator, applies snapshot to DiffableDataSource. .empty hides indicator and tableView, shows emptyStateView. .error(message) hides indicator, shows errorView with message. Every case must be handled — the compiler enforces exhaustiveness. Never use if-else chains on separate boolean properties.

assertions.text: Uses switch (not if-else chains) over ViewState in the sink
assertions.rationale: Exhaustive switch is the skill-prescribed approach — compiler enforces all cases are handled

assertions.text: Handles all four cases: loading, loaded, empty, error
assertions.rationale: All cases must be handled — non-exhaustive switch is a violation

assertions.text: Binding placed in viewDidLoad (not init or viewWillAppear)
assertions.rationale: Skill rule: bind in viewDidLoad

assertions.text: Uses hide-all-then-show reset pattern: hides all state views before the switch, then shows only the relevant view per case
assertions.rationale: Skill prescribes deterministic reset before switch to prevent stale UI from previous state — baseline typically sets visibility per-case without a reset step

assertions.text: Uses DiffableDataSource snapshot apply (applySnapshot) for .loaded — explicitly warns against tableView.reloadData()
assertions.rationale: Skill mandates snapshot-based updates when using DiffableDataSource — baseline often uses reloadData() or omits DiffableDataSource entirely

evals.name: viewstate-medium
evals.expected_output: Mixing DiffableDataSource with tableView.reloadData() is forbidden — calling reloadData() while using a DiffableDataSource corrupts the diffing state and can cause assertion failures or visual glitches. All table mutations must go through snapshot apply. Fix for updateSingleItem: use `snapshot.reconfigureItems([item])` (iOS 15+) which triggers cell reconfiguration without replacing cells, preserving animations. For iOS 14 and below, use `snapshot.reloadItems([item])`. Apply the updated snapshot via dataSource?.apply(snapshot, animatingDifferences: false).

assertions.text: Identifies tableView.reloadData() while using DiffableDataSource as forbidden — corrupts diffing state
assertions.rationale: Mixing reloadData with DiffableDataSource is a specific UIKit constraint from viewcontroller-lifecycle.md

assertions.text: Recommends snapshot.reconfigureItems (iOS 15+) or reloadItems for single-item updates
assertions.rationale: reconfigureItems is the correct DiffableDataSource update API — baseline typically only knows reloadData

evals.name: viewstate-complex
evals.expected_output: Two issues: (1) Binding in viewWillAppear — each time the view reappears (e.g., after returning from a detail screen) a new subscription is added to cancellables. Over N appearances there are N duplicate subscriptions all firing. Move bindings to viewDidLoad which runs exactly once. If fresh data is needed on appear, call viewModel.refresh() in viewWillAppear without re-subscribing. (2) Non-exhaustive switch — .empty and .error cases are missing. The compiler will warn or error (in Swift 6). Add all cases; unhandled ViewState cases leave the UI in an undefined state.

assertions.text: Identifies binding in viewWillAppear accumulates duplicate subscriptions on each appearance
assertions.rationale: viewWillAppear binding duplication is a specific lifecycle mistake from viewcontroller-lifecycle.md

assertions.text: Recommends moving bindings to viewDidLoad; calling viewModel.refresh() in viewWillAppear for fresh data
assertions.rationale: Correct separation: subscribe once in viewDidLoad, trigger data reload in viewWillAppear

assertions.text: Identifies non-exhaustive switch — .empty and .error cases missing
assertions.rationale: ViewState enum must be handled exhaustively

evals.name: testing-simple
evals.expected_output: Use XCTestExpectation + sink. Pattern: (1) Create MockItemService with stubbed response, (2) Create sut = ItemViewModel(service: mockService), (3) Create expectation, (4) Subscribe: viewModel.$state.dropFirst().sink { if case .loaded = $0 { expectation.fulfill() } }.store(in: &cancellables), (5) sut.fetch(), (6) await fulfillment(of: [expectation], timeout: 1.0). Note: dropFirst() skips the initial .loading emission if testing the subsequent state; omit dropFirst() if testing the sequence. Use await fulfillment(of:) — never wait(for:) in async tests.

assertions.text: Uses XCTestExpectation + sink to test the @Published state publisher
assertions.rationale: Core Combine testing pattern from testing.md

assertions.text: Uses protocol mock (MockItemService) injected via constructor
assertions.rationale: Testing through injected protocol mock

assertions.text: Uses await fulfillment(of:) — not wait(for:)
assertions.rationale: Skill explicitly warns: use await fulfillment(of:) in async tests, wait(for:) deadlocks — baseline often uses wait(for:)

evals.name: testing-medium
evals.expected_output: Missing memory leak detection. Add `addTeardownBlock { [weak sut] in XCTAssertNil(sut) }` in setUp immediately after creating sut. This verifies sut is actually deallocated when set to nil in tearDown, catching retain cycles that would otherwise silently persist between test runs. Without it, a retain cycle in the ViewModel (e.g., a stored closure capturing self strongly) would never be caught by the test suite.

assertions.text: Identifies missing memory leak detection
assertions.rationale: Memory leak detection is a required part of testing setup from testing.md

assertions.text: Adds addTeardownBlock { [weak sut] in XCTAssertNil(sut) } in setUp after creating sut
assertions.rationale: Exact addTeardownBlock pattern from testing.md — baseline typically uses deinit or manual weak reference checks, not addTeardownBlock

assertions.text: Explains this catches retain cycles that are otherwise silent (sut would survive tearDown)
assertions.rationale: Explains why this pattern is necessary

evals.name: testing-complex
evals.expected_output: wait(for:) in an async test context blocks the current thread (the cooperative thread pool thread running the async test). If the publisher emits on the same thread via the cooperative pool, the emission can never happen while wait(for:) blocks. This causes an intermittent deadlock. Fix: replace `wait(for: [expectation], timeout: 2.0)` with `await fulfillment(of: [expectation], timeout: 2.0)`. The await suspends the test coroutine without blocking the thread, allowing the publisher to emit. Additionally, if the sink closure captures self (the test class), add [weak self] to prevent a retain cycle.

assertions.text: Identifies wait(for:) blocks the cooperative thread in an async test context — causes deadlock
assertions.rationale: Skill explicitly warns: never use wait(for:) in async contexts — deadlocks cooperative pool; baseline often accepts wait(for:) as valid

assertions.text: Fixes with await fulfillment(of: [expectation], timeout: 2.0)
assertions.rationale: await fulfillment(of:) is the XCTest API that suspends without blocking — skill-specific knowledge

assertions.text: Explains await fulfillment suspends the coroutine without blocking the thread, allowing emission
assertions.rationale: Explains why the fix works mechanically

evals.name: refactoring-simple
evals.prompt: How should you start refactoring a 1,500-line MassiveViewController to MVVM?
evals.expected_output: Follow the phased PR approach from the skill: (1) Create a refactoring/ directory with a per-feature plan file (e.g., refactoring/profile-screen.md) listing every issue with Location, Severity, Problem, and Fix — not just titles. (2) Phase 1 PR (≤200 lines, single concern): fix critical safety issues only — retain cycles, threading bugs, force-unwraps. These ship first because they are low-risk and high-value. (3) Phase 2: extract ViewModel — move state properties and business logic. (4) Phase 3: add Combine bindings. (5) Phase 4: add Coordinator. New findings discovered during refactoring go to refactoring/discovered.md, not into the current PR.

assertions.text: Recommends creating a refactoring/ directory with per-feature plan files
assertions.rationale: refactoring/ directory protocol is the skill's prescribed workflow — baseline typically goes straight to code changes

assertions.text: States Phase 1 PR = critical safety issues only, ≤200 lines
assertions.rationale: ≤200 lines per PR and safety-first ordering are explicit skill constraints — baseline may suggest doing everything at once

assertions.text: Notes new discoveries go to refactoring/discovered.md, not into the current PR
assertions.rationale: Scope discipline: current PR stays focused, discoveries are tracked for later

assertions.text: Provides an anti-patterns checklist with severity levels (Critical/High/Medium) for scanning the VC before writing the plan
assertions.rationale: Skill prescribes a severity-ranked scanning checklist (force-unwraps=Critical, no ViewModel=High, reloadData=Medium) — baseline typically lists issues without severity classification

assertions.text: Includes a PR size reference table with maximum line counts per phase (e.g., Phase 1 ≤200, Phase 3 ≤150)
assertions.rationale: Skill provides concrete per-phase line limits in a table — baseline may mention small PRs without quantifying per phase

evals.name: refactoring-medium
evals.prompt:
This PR claims to be 'Phase 1 of ProfileViewController refactor'. What's wrong with its scope?

```
PR: Refactor ProfileViewController — Phase 1
Changed files (847 lines diff):
- Extract ProfileViewModel (state + networking)
- Add Combine @Published bindings
- Add ProfileCoordinator for navigation
- Migrate to DiffableDataSource
- Add constructor injection
- Fix force-unwrap on line 142
- Fix retain cycle on line 89
- Add ProfileViewModelTests
```
evals.expected_output: PR scope violates the phased refactoring rule — 847 lines, 8 concerns mixed together. A Phase 1 PR should be ≤200 lines and a single concern. Correct breakdown: Phase 1 (≤200 lines): fix force-unwrap + retain cycle — critical safety issues only, low risk, fast to review. Phase 2: extract ProfileViewModel. Phase 3: add Combine bindings. Phase 4: add Coordinator. Phase 5: DiffableDataSource. Phase 6: tests. Each phase is independently reviewable and revertable. A large mixed PR is hard to review and risky to merge — one bug in bindings requires reverting all safety fixes.

assertions.text: Identifies PR is too large (847 lines) and mixes too many concerns
assertions.rationale: ≤200 lines per PR is the skill's hard constraint

assertions.text: States Phase 1 should contain only critical safety fixes (force-unwrap + retain cycle)
assertions.rationale: Safety-first phase ordering is the skill's explicit rule — other concerns are not Phase 1

assertions.text: Shows correct phase breakdown across 5-6 separate PRs
assertions.rationale: Skill's phased approach explicitly separates each concern into its own PR — baseline often merges phases 2-3

assertions.text: Explains the revert risk: a bug in one concern (e.g., Coordinator) forces reverting all other changes (safety fixes, ViewModel) in a mega-PR
assertions.rationale: Skill explicitly describes the revert scenario as motivation for phased PRs — baseline typically says 'hard to review' without the revert consequence

evals.name: refactoring-complex
evals.expected_output: Three-step strategy: (1) Keep existing tests green throughout the migration — Phase 1 and 2 PRs must not break them. Don't delete old VC internals until the ViewModel covers the same behavior. (2) In parallel with ViewModel extraction (Phase 2), write new ViewModel tests that test behavior through the public interface (no UIKit): `XCTAssertEqual(sut.state, .loaded(expectedUser))`. These don't touch the VC. (3) Once ViewModel tests pass and cover the same behaviors, migrate the UITests to test via ViewModel or use XCUITest for end-to-end flows. Only then remove old VC internals. Log the UITest migration as a task in refactoring/discovered.md, not in the current Phase 2 PR.

assertions.text: States existing tests must stay green throughout the migration — don't break them during VM extraction
assertions.rationale: Production-first rule: stability over speed — skill explicitly states keep tests green

assertions.text: Recommends writing ViewModel tests (no UIKit) in parallel before removing VC internals
assertions.rationale: Test coverage must exist before removing old code

assertions.text: Logs UITest migration to refactoring/discovered.md as a separate task
assertions.rationale: Skill's scope discipline: current PR stays focused; discovered work goes to refactoring/discovered.md

evals.name: anti-patterns-simple
evals.expected_output: 🔴 Critical — networking in ViewController. Business logic (network call, JSON decoding) in the VC makes it completely untestable without a live network. This is the highest-severity violation in the anti-patterns checklist. Fix: extract to a ViewModel with an injected service protocol. The VC only observes @Published state and renders. This is always Phase 1 or Phase 2 in a refactoring plan — never left for later.

assertions.text: Flags as Critical (🔴) severity
assertions.rationale: Skill ranks networking-in-VC as Critical — highest severity in anti-patterns.md

assertions.text: Explains this makes the VC completely untestable without live network
assertions.rationale: Core reason networking belongs in ViewModel

assertions.text: States this must be addressed in Phase 1 or 2 — not deferred
assertions.rationale: Critical violations ship first in the phased workflow

evals.name: anti-patterns-medium
evals.expected_output: Three violations ranked by severity: (1) 🔴 Critical: CartViewModel holds CartViewController reference — creates a retain cycle (VC → VM → VC), ViewModel owns a UIKit object, and ViewModel calls UI method (dismiss). This is the most severe: a leak + UIKit coupling + navigation-in-ViewModel. Fix: use a navigation closure (`var onCheckoutComplete: (() -> Void)?`) that the Coordinator sets; ViewModel calls `onCheckoutComplete?()`. (2) 🟡 High: viewModel is optional var — allows nil at runtime, should be non-optional via constructor injection. (3) 🟢 Medium: checkout() calls dismiss directly — navigation belongs in Coordinator via a closure.

assertions.text: Flags CartViewController reference in ViewModel as Critical — retain cycle + UIKit coupling + navigation in VM
assertions.rationale: VM↔VC retain cycle via strong reference is a top-severity violation from anti-patterns.md

assertions.text: Recommends navigation closure (onCheckoutComplete?) set by Coordinator instead of dismiss in VM
assertions.rationale: Navigation closure pattern is the skill's specific fix — not a delegate or notification

assertions.text: Flags optional viewModel var as High — should be non-optional constructor injection
assertions.rationale: Optional force-unwrapped or silently-nil dependencies are a High violation

evals.name: anti-patterns-complex
evals.expected_output: Five violations: (1) 🔴 Critical: Singleton ViewController (static shared = self) — global mutable state, memory leak (self never deallocates), multi-window unsafe, breaks navigation. Remove entirely. (2) 🔴 Critical: Networking in ViewController (loadItems calling APIService.shared) — untestable, hidden dependency. Extract to ViewModel. (3) 🔴 Critical: VC instantiating and pushing another VC in didSelectRowAt — navigation must be in Coordinator via ViewModel closure. (4) 🟡 High: No ViewModel, no ViewState enum — separate isLoading boolean, raw state in VC. (5) 🟢 Medium: UITableViewDataSource in VC with reloadData — should use DiffableDataSource with a dedicated data source. File should also be flagged if it exceeds 400 lines.

assertions.text: Flags static shared ViewController as Critical — global state, memory leak, multi-window unsafe
assertions.rationale: Singleton VC is a top-severity violation from anti-patterns.md — baseline may miss the multi-window issue

assertions.text: Flags networking in VC as Critical and VC-to-VC navigation as Critical
assertions.rationale: Both are top-severity violations — must be in Phase 1/2 refactoring

assertions.text: Correctly ranks 3 Critical violations before 1 High (no ViewState) and 1 Medium (DiffableDataSource)
assertions.rationale: Severity ranking from anti-patterns.md is skill-specific — baseline often flags all issues equally

assertions.text: Recommends ViewModel extraction and Coordinator for navigation as the architectural fixes
assertions.rationale: Concrete fix direction for the critical violations
